__data__.text:
CASE: Ghaziabad Development Authority v. Balbir Singh
YEAR: 2004
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: (2004) 5 SCC 65
CATEGORY: Consumer / Real Estate / Deficiency of Service
RULING: The Supreme Court upheld the award of compensation to a flat buyer for delay in possession by the Ghaziabad Development Authority. Held that allottees are consumers under the Consumer Protection Act and delay in handing over possession constitutes deficiency of service. Interest at 18% per annum on deposited amounts was awarded.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Delay in possession entitles flat buyers to compensation and interest under the Consumer Protection Act. Builder/Authority cannot retain money without delivering the property.

CASE: Lucknow Development Authority v. M.K. Gupta
YEAR: 1993
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 1994 SC 787
CATEGORY: Consumer / Housing / Deficiency of Service
RULING: Landmark judgment holding that public authorities providing housing are subject to the Consumer Protection Act. Deficiency of service by government bodies is actionable. The Court also held that Consumer Forums can award compensation for mental agony and harassment.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Government housing authorities are liable as service providers under the Consumer Protection Act. Mental agony is compensable.

CASE: Spring Meadows Hospital v. Harjol Ahluwalia
YEAR: 1998
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: (1998) 4 SCC 39
CATEGORY: Consumer / Medical Negligence
RULING: The Supreme Court held that medical negligence resulting in death of a child entitles the parents to compensation under the Consumer Protection Act. A nurse administering wrong medication and a doctor failing to supervise constituted gross negligence. Parents, as dependants, are consumers even though they themselves did not avail the service.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Medical negligence is actionable under the Consumer Protection Act. Dependants of the patient are also consumers.

CASE: National Insurance Co. Ltd v. Hindustan Safety Glass Works Ltd
YEAR: 2017
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: (2017) 5 SCC 781
CATEGORY: Consumer / Insurance / Deficiency of Service
RULING: The Supreme Court held that an insurance company repudiating a valid claim without sufficient reason constitutes deficiency of service under the Consumer Protection Act. The insurer is bound by the terms of the policy and cannot impose conditions not mentioned therein to reject a claim.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Unjustified repudiation of insurance claims is deficiency of service. Insurer must settle claims in accordance with policy terms.

CASE: Emaar MGF Land Ltd v. Aftab Singh
YEAR: 2018
COURT: National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission
CITATION: RP No. 4086 of 2017
CATEGORY: Consumer / Real Estate / RERA
RULING: The NCDRC held that a consumer can file a complaint before the Consumer Forum even after RERA came into force, as the remedies are concurrent and not exclusive. Builder liable for delay in possession and must refund with 12% interest or offer possession with compensation.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Consumer Forum and RERA jurisdiction is concurrent; buyer can choose either forum. Interest at 12% for delayed possession.

CASE: Indra Sarma v. V.K.V. Sarma
YEAR: 2013
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: (2013) 15 SCC 755
CATEGORY: Domestic Violence / Protection of Women
RULING: The Supreme Court held that a woman in a live-in relationship that resembles marriage is entitled to protection under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005. The 'relationship in the nature of marriage' includes long-term live-in relationships and the woman can claim residence rights, maintenance, and protection orders.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Live-in relationships of a matrimonial nature fall within the Domestic Violence Act. Women can claim protection orders, residence orders, and monetary relief.

CASE: Aruna Parmod Shah v. Union of India
YEAR: 2008
COURT: Delhi High Court
CITATION: WP(C) No. 7183/2007
CATEGORY: Domestic Violence / Residence Rights
RULING: The Delhi High Court upheld the constitutional validity of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 and held that a woman has the right to reside in the shared household regardless of whether she has ownership rights. The husband cannot forcibly evict her without obtaining a court order under the Act.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Women have an inalienable right to reside in the matrimonial home. Forcible eviction is an act of domestic violence.

CASE: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
YEAR: 2015
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 2015 SC 1523
CATEGORY: Cyber / IT Act / Free Speech
RULING: The Supreme Court struck down Section 66A of the IT Act 2000 as unconstitutional for being vague and overbroad, violating Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech). However, Sections 69A (blocking of websites) and 79 (intermediary liability) were upheld subject to procedural safeguards.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Section 66A of the IT Act is void. Online speech can only be restricted under valid reasonable restrictions in Article 19(2). Victims of online fraud should use Section 66D (cheating by personation using computer) instead.

CASE: State of Tamil Nadu v. Suhas Katti
YEAR: 2004
COURT: Additional Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Chennai
CITATION: Case No. 4680/2004
CATEGORY: Cyber / Harassment / IT Act
RULING: First conviction under the IT Act 2000. The accused was convicted under Section 67 (publishing obscene material electronically) and Sections 469 and 509 of the IPC for sending obscene messages and posting the victim's personal information online to harass her. Sentenced to rigorous imprisonment.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Online harassment including posting private information and obscene content is punishable under Section 67 IT Act and Section 509 IPC. Cybercrime police can investigate and arrest.

CASE: Infosys BPO Ltd v. Shamsher Martinez
YEAR: 2009
COURT: Madras High Court
CITATION: CS No. 189/2009
CATEGORY: IP / Trade Secret / IT Act
RULING: The Madras High Court granted an injunction restraining a former employee from using or disclosing confidential client data downloaded from the company's systems before resignation. Unauthorized data download from an employer's system constitutes an offence under Section 43 and Section 66 of the IT Act 2000.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Unauthorized access to and download of employer data is an IT Act offence. Injunctions available to protect trade secrets and confidential information.

CASE: Time Incorporated v. Lokesh Srivastava
YEAR: 2005
COURT: Delhi High Court
CITATION: CS(OS) No. 2172/2004
CATEGORY: IP / Copyright / Trademark
RULING: The Delhi High Court held that passing off and trademark infringement are distinct torts. The defendant used a mark similar to TIME magazine's mark for a publication. Punitive damages of Rs 5 lakh were awarded as deterrence even though actual damages were difficult to prove, establishing the principle of punitive damages in IP cases in India.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Punitive damages are available in Indian trademark and copyright cases. Similarity causing confusion in the marketplace is actionable even without proof of actual loss.

CASE: Workmen of Dimakuchi Tea Estate v. Management of Dimakuchi Tea Estate
YEAR: 1958
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 1958 SC 353
CATEGORY: Labour / Wages
RULING: The Supreme Court held that workers are entitled to wages for the period of unlawful termination. Employers cannot withhold wages due to pending disciplinary proceedings. Section 3 of the Payment of Wages Act mandates timely payment and Section 15 provides the remedy for recovery.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Wages are a fundamental entitlement; delay in payment attracts compensation up to 10x the unpaid amount under Section 15 of the Payment of Wages Act 1936.

CASE: Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Ltd v. Brojo Nath Ganguly
YEAR: 1986
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 1986 SC 1571
CATEGORY: Labour / Unfair Dismissal
RULING: The Supreme Court struck down a service rule that gave the employer unilateral power to terminate employment with 3-months' notice, holding it unconscionable and violative of Article 14. Workers cannot be dismissed without just cause and due inquiry.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Arbitrary termination clauses in employment contracts are void. Workers dismissed without inquiry are entitled to reinstatement or back wages under the Industrial Disputes Act.

CASE: Surendra Kumar Verma v. Central Government Industrial Tribunal
YEAR: 1980
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 1981 SC 422
CATEGORY: Labour / Industrial Dispute
RULING: Held that an employer cannot declare a lockout without following the procedure under the Industrial Disputes Act. Workers are entitled to wages during an illegal lockout period. Section 22 of the Industrial Disputes Act requires 14 days' notice before a lockout in public utility services.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Illegal lockouts entitle workmen to full wages during the lockout period. Employer must follow mandatory notice requirements.

CASE: State of U.P. v. Synthetics and Chemicals Ltd
YEAR: 1991
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 1991 SC 1672
CATEGORY: Labour / Contract Workers
RULING: Contract workers performing the same work as permanent employees are entitled to equal pay under Article 21 and the Equal Remuneration Act 1976. Differential wage structures for identical work constitute unfair labour practice.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Equal pay for equal work applies to contract workers. Discrimination in wage structure for same job violates fundamental rights.

CASE: Hussainbhai Calicut v. The Alath Factory Thozhilali Union
YEAR: 1978
COURT: Supreme Court of India
CITATION: AIR 1978 SC 1410
CATEGORY: Labour / Employer-Employee Relationship
RULING: The Court held that for the purposes of the Industrial Disputes Act, the test of who is the real employer is functional, not merely contractual. Sub-contracted workers working on the principal employer's premises under their supervision are employees for all practical purposes.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Functional test determines employer-employee relationship; sub-contracted workers enjoy full labour law protections.
